preFix.title: Fitting brush pile draught seal to a sash window
preFix.subtitle: Seal the vertical and horizontal gaps on a sliding sash window
preFix.excerpt: How to fit self-adhesive brush pile draught-seal strips to the channels and meeting rail of a traditional sliding sash window to reduce draughts and heat loss, without restricting the sash from sliding.
preFix.sourceNotes: Energy Saving Trust draught-proofing guidance. Preservation and Retrofit Guide, Historic England. Collins Complete DIY Manual.

content.text: A traditional sliding sash window has four main draught paths: the two vertical side channels where the sashes run, the
where upper and lower sashes overlap, and the outer sill edge of the lower sash.

is the right product for sash windows because it allows the sash to slide while still closing the gap. Foam compression seals cannot be used in the channels: they would seize the sash after the first cold night.
Step 1, clean and prepare the channel surfaces

Open both sashes and slide them to give access to the full run of each channel. Scrape old paint, dust, and debris from the parting bead faces and the faces of the inner staff bead using the 50 mm scraper. Lightly sand the surfaces with the sanding block and wipe clean with a dry cloth. The self-adhesive seal will only stick reliably to a clean, dry surface: any paint contamination shortens its life.
Step 2, measure and cut the pile seal

Measure the full run height of each channel from the sill to the head. Cut a length of pile seal to match with the utility knife and a straight edge. Cut cleanly square: a ragged end pushes debris into the channel as the sash moves. For the meeting rail gap, measure the width of the sash and cut accordingly. Four lengths are needed: two for the side channels, one for the face of the upper sash meeting rail, one for the face of the lower sash meeting rail.
Step 3, fit the pile seal in the channels
Peel the backing from the pile seal and press it firmly onto the face of the

in each channel, positioning it so that the pile faces inward toward the sash face. Work from top to bottom, pressing along the full length. The pile should gently brush against the sash face when the sash slides: compress it slightly with a finger to ensure contact without gripping. On the inner channel (between the lower sash and the staff bead), a second strip may be needed if the gap is wide.
Step 4, fit the seal at the meeting rail

Close both sashes and examine the gap at the meeting rail. Peel and press one length of pile seal to the horizontal face of the upper sash's bottom rail: the face that presses against the lower sash when closed. Press another length on the horizontal top face of the lower sash's top rail. When the window is closed, the two pile seals compress gently against each other to close the gap. Open and close the window to check: both sashes should slide freely without dragging.

The outer edge of the lower sash rests on the window sill when closed. This gap is usually sealed with a foam compression seal rather than pile: peel and press a 6 mm self-adhesive foam seal strip onto the window sill in the path of the sash, so that the sash compresses the foam when closed. Check that the sash still closes fully: if the foam is too thick it will hold the sash open slightly.

Mohair pile seal is more durable and compressible than polypropylene. It is the preferred product for listed or heritage sash windows where the draughtproofing must not change the look of the frame. Professional restorers often remove the parting bead, rout a channel into its face, and press the pile seal into the channel flush with the surface. This is a neater result but requires removing and refitting the beads.

attrs.fix: Remove the seal, clean the channel, and select a thinner pile product. Pile seals come in widths of 4 mm, 6 mm, and 9 mm — start with 4 mm and move up only if the gap demands it.
attrs.cause: Pile seal too thick for the gap; adhesive applied to a damp or painted surface and the seal is peeling and bunching.
attrs.symptom: Sash stiff or dragging after fitting the pile seal

attrs.fix: Strip the old seal, clean the surface with methylated spirit, allow to dry, and press new seal firmly along its full length.
attrs.cause: Surface was greasy, damp, or painted when the seal was applied.
attrs.symptom: Seal peels off the channel within a few weeks

attrs.fix: Check that the meeting rail pile seals are making contact when the window is closed. Check the sill compression seal is thick enough. Check that the window latch or fitch fastener is holding the sashes tightly shut.
attrs.cause: Gap at the meeting rail or sill not sealed; sash not closing fully against the seal.
attrs.symptom: Cold draught persists despite the seal being in place

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
